reactions.label: documented
reactions.source: PDSZ Program 2024; PDSZ 2026 statements (retain 6/8-year tracks; reduce segregation; reform admissions)
reactions.interest: Classroom teachers and other pedagogical staff in state, church, and foundation schools organized by PDSZ
reactions.public_good_frame: Equitable access to public education achieved without degrading teachers' working conditions, professional autonomy, or job security
reactions.argument: S1's mechanism — reforming the admission assessment and introducing SES-weighted quotas while leaving the 6/8-year tracks themselves untouched — implements two of the three planks of PDSZ's documented 2024/2026 programme (reduce segregation, reform admissions) without touching the third (keep the tracks). This is the closest match among the four scenarios to our stated position.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support would lapse into conditional or opposed if the new assessment design, compliance-audit unit, or quota administration is imposed on schools without paid teacher time, training, and consultation with union representatives, since the scenario's own cost categories list this administrative burden without specifying who absorbs it.

reactions.label: documented
reactions.interest: Classroom teachers and other pedagogical staff currently employed at 6/8-year gimnáziums whose entry places would shrink under the cap trajectory
reactions.public_good_frame: Stable, adequately staffed public education that does not solve segregation by eliminating teaching positions and forcing staff transitions
reactions.argument: PDSZ's documented programme commits to keeping the 6/8-year tracks; S2's pre-announced cap trajectory is a scheduled, gradual abolition of exactly those entry places. The scenario's felmenő rendszer protects already-enrolled pupils but its implementation steps say nothing about protecting the jobs, workload, or pay of teachers at the schools whose intake shrinks each year.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would reconsider only if the trajectory were paired with binding job-security and redeployment guarantees for affected teaching staff and a resourcing commitment to general-school advanced programmes that produces no net loss of teaching positions or pay — neither is currently specified in the scenario.

reactions.label: documented
reactions.interest: Classroom teachers across the general and 6/8-year gimnázium system who would face mandatory retraining and institutional conversion
reactions.public_good_frame: Structural reform of public education should not be financed through unresourced demands on the teaching workforce
reactions.argument: S3 abolishes the 6/8-year entry points outright, directly contradicting PDSZ's documented commitment to retain them. Beyond that core conflict, the scenario's own cost categories list 'high' nationwide teacher retraining for differentiated instruction with no numeric estimate, and its implementation steps schedule national conversion (years 6-8) without a described contingency if the cross-party pact needed to protect the reform from reversal fails.
reactions.condition_to_change: Opposition would only be revisited given a binding negotiated agreement with the teaching profession guaranteeing job security, fully paid and resourced retraining time, and workload protection through the multi-year transition — none of which is part of the scenario as specified or of PDSZ's current documented position.

reactions.label: documented
reactions.source: PDSZ Program 2024; PDSZ 2026 statements (retain 6/8-year tracks; reduce segregation; reform admissions)
reactions.interest: Teachers in disadvantaged general schools who would receive funding premia and support packages, and the profession broadly regarding credibility of pay promises under a teacher shortage
reactions.public_good_frame: Compensation for disadvantaged schools is welcome only if it is durable and matched by real measures to address teacher supply, not treated as a substitute for admissions reform
reactions.argument: S4 retains the 6/8-year tracks and funds a segregation-reduction package, matching two of PDSZ's three documented planks, but on its own it omits the admissions-reform plank our programme also requires, so it is a partial rather than full match. The scenario's own political-risk analysis further cites PDSZ/PSZ survey evidence that Hungary's teacher shortage constrains the credibility of premia and support-package promises — a concern that falls directly within our documented remit on teacher pay and working conditions.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would move to full support if paired with the admissions reform described in S1, and would move to opposition if the compensation budget line is not protected from first-round fiscal cuts or if premia are announced without concrete measures to address the underlying teacher shortage, since a defunded or unstaffed package leaves the underlying inequity unaddressed while teachers absorb the workload of an under-resourced assignment.
